clean_story:
Earth’s hidden compass. Animals sense invisible fields.

The term magnetoreception merges "magnetic" and "reception"—the ability to detect Earth’s magnetic field, like reading a map printed in iron filings. Birds don’t just fly south for winter; they see the planet’s magnetic lines as overlapping layers of light and shadow. A robin in a windowless lab, blindfolded, will still turn its head to align with magnetic north.

Here’s how it works: Inside a pigeon’s beak, iron-rich nerves act like microscopic needles, twisting when the field shifts. Sea turtles hatchlings use the field’s angle to plot a straight-line swim toward the ocean, even from a nest they’ve never left. Salmon remember the magnetic "signature" of their birth river, returning years later with GPS-like precision.

Tests show cows and deer instinctively face north while grazing—no one knows why. The mechanism remains debated: some species may use quantum reactions in their eyes, where light triggers chemical compasses. One fact is clear: Strip away all landmarks, stars, or smells, and many animals will still find their way home.
Three related subjects are quantum biology secrets, animal migration mysteries, and human senses we lack.
